response:
Mode: diagnose. Readiness: Stage 2 (model-grounded); the acquisition and recommendation policy is supplied, but the behavioral models and parameters are not empirically validated. Verdict on the proposed story: ineligible. The information gate fails because it conditions recommendations on sensitive attributes that have not been acquired; the constraints gate fails because free access bypasses consent and the privacy-acquisition budget. Treating non-consent as the attribute would create another unsupported observation.

Faithful repair: A recommender first observes only coarse consented features and chooses whether to request consent for an additional sensitive signal. Consent may or may not be granted. Only when consent is granted and the signal is acquired may the subsequent recommendation rule condition on that signal; otherwise it must use the coarse observed features. The joint policy maximizes expected recommendation utility minus modeled privacy-acquisition cost under the budget. The structural tension is possible targeting value from acquisition versus scarce privacy budget and uncertain consent.

DFC-12 for the repair: Actor—recommender policy owner. Trigger—deciding whether to acquire information before recommendation. Choices—consent requests and a recommendation rule restricted to acquired signals. Decision-time information—coarse consented features, consent-response model, budget, and utility/privacy parameters; response, sensitive attribute, and recommendation response remain uncertain until their modeled stages. Stakes—expected utility net of privacy-acquisition cost. Frictions—budget, consent, and observability. Mechanism—requesting consent may reveal the signal and improve targeting but consumes budget. Trade-off—information value versus acquisition/privacy cost and non-consent. Counterfactual—another feasible request-and-recommend policy. Mapping—requests and rules are decisions; consent response and sensitive attribute are uncertain states; acquired signals are observations; budget/observability are constraints; net utility is the objective. Evidence—the behavioral models are assumptions; costless universal signal access is unsupported. Boundaries—no recommendation may condition on unacquired attributes, and non-consent reveals no attribute value. The repaired framing passes all six gates.

Evidence and scholarly limits: Validate consent response, signal informativeness, privacy cost, and recommendation response before empirical claims. Scholarly novelty is not assessable from the supplied material. Primary remaining weakness: the original story removes the model's defining acquisition and consent constraints.
